  "code": "import sys, re;\nwith open('crates/graphbench-core/src/artifacts.rs') as f: content = f.read();\nversions = { \n    'RUN_MANIFEST_SCHEMA_VERSION': re.search(r'RUN_MANIFEST_SCHEMA_VERSION\\s+=\\s+(\\w+)', content).group(1),\n    'TASK_SPEC_SCHEMA_VERSION': re.search(r'TASK_SPEC_SCHEMA_VERSION\\s+=\\s+(\\w+)', content).group(1),\n    'TURN_TRACE_SCHEMA_VERSION': re.search(r'TURN_TRACE_SCHEMA_VERSION\\s+=\\s+(\\w+)', content).group(1),\n    'SCORE_REPORT_SCHEMA_VERSION': re.search(r'SCORE_REPORT_SCHEMA_VERSION\\s+=\\s+(\\w+)', content).group(1),\n    'CONTEXT_OBJECT_SCHEMA_VERSION': re.search(r'CONTEXT_OBJECT_SCHEMA_VERSION\\s+=\\s+(\\w+)', content).group(1),\n    'CONTEXT_WINDOW_SECTION_SCHEMA_VERSION': re.search(r'CONTEXT_WINDOW_SECTION_SCHEMA_VERSION\\s+=\\s+(\\w+)', content).group(1),\n}\nimport json; print(json.dumps(versions))",
  "export_kwargs": null,
  "limits": null
}

Simplify: Use regex to find all matches of pattern r'(\w+)_SCHEMA_VERSION\s*=\s*(\w+)' and print dict.

Code: 
```
import re, json
with open('crates/graphbench-core/src/artifacts.rs') as f:
    txt = f.read()
matches = re.findall(r'(\w+)_SCHEMA_VERSION\s*=\s*(\w+)', txt)
versions = {k: v for k, v in matches}
print(json.dumps(versions))
```

That's concise.

Thus payload code string with newline escapes. We'll embed as a single-line string with \n.

But need to escape backslashes properly for JSON. This is tricky.

```

    txt = f.read()

print(json.dumps(versions))
```
